title: Range-Based Loops
description: Formalizing the for (x : container) syntax previewed throughout the STL topics — what it desugars to, and the crucial value vs reference choice.
objectives: Write a range-based for loop correctly
Understand what it actually desugars to under the hood
Choose between by-value, by-reference, and const-reference correctly
Know it works on any container with begin()/end()

sections.text: 1. Recap: We've Been Previewing This All Along
for (int n : numbers) has shown up as an unexplained preview since set. It's time to explain it properly.
Read for (int n : numbers) as "for each int n in numbers." No index, no begin()/end(), no *it — it handles the entire traversal automatically.

This is the exact begin()/end()/*it/it++ pattern from STL Iterators, just written for you automatically by the compiler. That's also why it works on any container that provides begin() and end() — vector, list, set, map, and even our std::array.

This is exactly Pass by Value vs Pass by Reference, applied to a loop variable. for (int n : numbers) gives n as a copy of each element — modifying n does nothing to the original. for (int &n : numbers) makes n an alias for the actual element (recall References), so modifying n genuinely modifies numbers.

For read-only traversal, const int &n avoids copying each element (efficient, especially for larger types like string or a class object) while also preventing accidental modification — genuinely the best default for a loop that only reads. Use plain int n only for genuinely small types (like int) where the copy is essentially free, and int &n specifically when you intend to modify the container.
6. Works on Any Container with begin()/end()
This is the exact same pattern used unexplained back in map's example — now fully justified. auto here is a preview of the very next topic, auto Keyword.

sections.title: "For each item on the list" instead of "check item 1, then item 2, then..."
sections.text: "For each book on the shelf, check its title" doesn't require counting positions or naming an index variable — you just handle each book in turn. A range-based for loop is exactly that natural phrasing in code, versus the manual position-tracking of an index-based or iterator-based loop.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using for (int n : numbers) and expecting modifications to n to change the container — that requires for (int &n : numbers) instead.
Copying large elements unnecessarily with by-value range-based loops — const auto& (or const Type&) avoids the copy for read-only access.
Modifying a container's size (like push_back()) from inside a range-based loop over it — this can invalidate the loop's underlying iterators, exactly the danger covered in Iterators.
Forgetting range-based loops need begin()/end() to exist — they work on all our STL containers, but wouldn't work on some custom type lacking those.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does a range-based for loop actually desugar to?
questions.answer: A regular loop using begin(), end(), dereferencing, and incrementing an iterator — the compiler writes that boilerplate automatically.

questions.question: What's the difference between for (int n : v) and for (int &n : v)?
questions.answer: The first gives n as a copy of each element, so modifying n doesn't affect v; the second makes n a reference to the actual element, so modifying n does modify v.

questions.question: Why is const auto& (or const Type&) often recommended for read-only range-based loops?
questions.answer: It avoids copying each element (efficient for larger types) while also preventing accidental modification, since the reference is const.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A range-based for loop, for (Type x : container), automatically handles the begin()/end()/*it/it++ pattern from STL Iterators, working on any container that provides begin() and end(). The value-vs-reference choice matters just like Pass by Value vs Pass by Reference: for (Type x : container) copies each element, so modifications don't affect the original, while for (Type &x : container) references the real elements, allowing modification. const Type& is usually the best default for read-only iteration, avoiding both an unnecessary copy and accidental changes.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Range-Based Loops
sections.text: for (x : container) = "for each x in container," auto-generated iterator loop underneath. Plain x = copy (safe, wasteful for big types). &x = the real thing (can modify). const &x = read the real thing, no copy, no accidents.
